elements.sent-2411-069: Qualitative coding, whether inductive or deductive, can also mechanically be seen as applying labels (i.e., codes) to a given piece of data.
elements.sent-2411-070: This section reviews two ML/GAI perspectives for machine-assisted qualitative coding - classification and generation.
elements.sent-2411-071: While classification naturally fits into deductive coding scenarios with established evaluation metrics, its potential for inductive coding is intrinsically limited.

elements.sent-2411-077: Rule-based AI systems that extract text-matching rules from human coding results and apply them to more datasets.
elements.sent-2411-078: For example, \cite{rietz_cody_2021 and \cite{gebreegziabher_patat_2023 encode human coders' coding results into rules for AI to suggest codes in unseen data.}}
elements.sent-2411-079: The usage of human-readable and editable rules increases the transparency in machine coding.
elements.sent-2411-080: LLM-based methods that instruct LLMs to label data with a predetermined codebook.
elements.sent-2411-081: For example, \cite{xiao_supporting_2023 used GPT-3 to label data with a fixed set of codes, thus supporting researchers in deductive coding.}
elements.sent-2411-082: Despite the increased explainability, some evaluation studies have pointed to increased bias as a potential setback\cite{ashwin2023using .}

elements.sent-2411-110: Following the suggestions of TA and GT, we adopt a team-based approach and measure individual coders' results against the aggregation of multiple coders.
elements.sent-2411-111: To achieve that, we proposed a conceptual structure, Code Space (CSP), to represent inductive codes produced by each individual.
elements.sent-2411-112: The sum of individual CSPs becomes an Aggregated Code Space (ACS).
elements.sent-2411-113: Using ACS as a reference for evaluation, we proposed four conceptual metrics (Coverage, Density, Novelty, and Divergence) to measure individual CSP's relative performance.

elements.sent-2411-114: Assuming we need to measure sets of inductive codes $Coding\_Results$.
elements.sent-2411-115: We define a Code Space (CSP) as a multi-dimensional conceptual space that covers multiple codes identified or interpreted from the underlying qualitative dataset, each representing a set of codes.
elements.sent-2411-116: Each code is represented by a network node and has multiple dimensions related to its conceptual nature.
elements.sent-2411-117: Combining multiple Code Spaces, we get a Aggregated Code Space (ACS) that covers all codes from all $Coding\_Results$ (Fig \ref{fig:acs-and-csp A).}
elements.sent-2411-118: Similar but not equivalent codes are now connected by links and considered as $Neighbors$.
elements.sent-2411-119: The idea of ACS acknowledges that humans may not find all possible interpretations but could still build an "aggregated" set of codes to come closer.
elements.sent-2411-120: ACS is also a snapshot in time that documents researchers’ current efforts.
elements.sent-2411-121: It can be used to look for convergence and divergence among the team members, supporting different stages of qualitative analysis.
elements.sent-2411-122: Convergence may help researchers determine the current consensus and indicate each code's visibility and importance.
elements.sent-2411-123: Divergence may reveal underlying biases, different focuses, or missed opportunities for researchers.
elements.sent-2411-124: For example, a recent study \cite{mcgaw_dissonance_2023 critiques overreliance on consensus building and advocates for more attention to “dissonances, disagreements, and differences.” The concepts of CSP and ACS enable us to measure inductive coding results $Coding\_Results$.}
elements.sent-2411-125: While this paper may have coined the term ACS, many qualitative researchers have long relied on similar ideas and practices (e.g., \citeauthor{thomas_general_2006 ).}
elements.sent-2411-126: Examination of ACS could happen during open coding, when researchers discuss what was discovered and not discovered by individual team members to help them reorient the next coding batch; during axial coding, when researchers collaborate to consolidate the ACS into codebooks; and during selective coding, when researchers truncate the ACS to coalesce around a core category relevant to the research question.
elements.sent-2411-127: We propose four conceptual metrics to measure individual CSPs against their aggregation ACS (Fig \ref{fig:acs-and-csp B), which will be operationalized in later sections:.}
elements.sent-2411-128: Coverage and Density: How much conceptual space does a CSP cover, and how many codes does it use to cover this space? Both TA and GT strive for "richness" of codes.
elements.sent-2411-129: In practice, researchers need to strive for breadth and depth.
elements.sent-2411-130: Breadth means covering as diverse sets of concepts as possible.
elements.sent-2411-131: Depth means the descriptive details that ensures the concept's meaningfulness and richness, thus supporting researchers' further analysis \cite{corbin_chapter_2008, braun2013successful .}
elements.sent-2411-132: Combining them, researchers may be able to capture findings with depth and variation, two criteria for evaluating GT studies \cite{corbin_chapter_2008 , to the extent that “nothing has been left out.”\cite{glaser2004remodeling On the other hand, not all codes are of equal interest to researchers.}}
elements.sent-2411-133: Some concepts are more likely to be grounded in or relevant to the research question.
elements.sent-2411-134: The metrics must account for each concept's importance and weigh it accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-135: Novelty: How much of the “novel” conceptual space does a CSP include? We define "novel" codes as ones that fewer than $Novelty\_Threshold$ CSPs have included.
elements.sent-2411-136: Most of the time, CSPs will not be (even close to) identical.
elements.sent-2411-137: Sometimes, a researcher brings in novel ideas or lenses that were missed by others; in other cases, a researcher could make a slip or inappropriately name a code.
elements.sent-2411-138: Identifying "novel" codes could help us identify either scenario and support human-human or human-AI collaboration.
elements.sent-2411-139: Divergence: How far is a CSP's code distribution from the ACS? Suppose two CSPs, A and B, have the same coverage and density.
elements.sent-2411-140: However, A has more codes in common with most CSPs.
elements.sent-2411-141: B, on the other hand, has more codes in common with no one else ("novel" codes) or few in the group.
elements.sent-2411-142: To detect this at the macro level, we calculate each CSP's divergence as the separation from its probability distribution of codes to that of the ACS, using the latter as a “ground truth.” In other words, the closer a CSP concept distribution is to the ACS, the less "divergent" it is.
elements.sent-2411-143: Since our evaluation focuses more on the relative comparison between coding results, it does not directly address potential issues of groundedness, i.e., whether codes could be reasonably re-identified by another coder from the underlying data.
elements.sent-2411-144: However, our metrics do provide an indirect pathway for human-AI collaboration: by looking at the "novel" parts of CSP and/or codes with very few examples, we may be able to identify codes with potential groundedness issues more easily.
elements.sent-2411-145: The operationalization of our method starts from consolidating $Coding\_Results$ into an ACS for evaluation reference.
elements.sent-2411-146: Each $Coding\_Result$ should come from an individual machine or human coder.
elements.sent-2411-147: For $Coding\_Result$ to be treated as a CSP, each $Code$ should have a $Label$, an optional list of $Definitions$, and a list of $Examples$ (i.e. the pieces of data where the code was identified).
elements.sent-2411-148: In the output ACS, each $Code$ will have a consolidated $Label$, $Definition$, a list of $Examples$, a list of $Neighbors$ (other codes that are semantically close to this one), and a list of $Owners$ ($Coding\_Results$ that have included one or more variations of the consolidated code).
elements.sent-2411-149: Evaluating individual CSPs against the ACS, we can calculate $Coverage$, $Density$, $Novelty$, and $Divergence$ as overall and cluster-level metrics.
elements.sent-2411-150: For anonymization reasons, we will open-source our implementation after the paper's acceptance.
elements.sent-2411-151: Overall, consolidating an ACS involves multiple iterations of:.
elements.sent-2411-152: Finding equivalent codes and merge them into one.
elements.sent-2411-153: Generate a new label and definition for merged codes.
elements.sent-2411-154: Repeat the process with the new list of codes, until nothing more is merged.
elements.sent-2411-155: Finding equivalent codes is more complicated than it seems.
elements.sent-2411-156: Human or machine coders often use different phrases to describe the same or similar ideas.
elements.sent-2411-157: For example, suppose coder A identified "user suggestion," while coder B found "user suggestions." They are clearly referring to the same idea.
elements.sent-2411-158: Suppose coder B found "user feedback" instead.
elements.sent-2411-159: Are A and B referring to equivalent, similar, or different idea(s)? To determine that:.
elements.sent-2411-160: We encode each code with text embedding, transforming its $Label$ and $Definition(s)$ into a high-dimensional vector.
elements.sent-2411-161: We use cosine distance, a commonly used text similarity measure, to calculate the distance between codes.
elements.sent-2411-162: We use a hierarchical clustering algorithm to choose merging candidates, since it does not specify the expected number of results.
elements.sent-2411-163: For each node in the algorithm's dendrogram tree structure, we apply two input distance thresholds: $lower$ and $upper$.. \begin{enumerate.}
elements.sent-2411-164: Different codes: Different codes with a distance above the $upper$ threshold will never be merged.
elements.sent-2411-165: Equivalent codes: Very similar codes with a distance below the $lower$ threshold will always be merged.
elements.sent-2411-166: For codes with a distance below the $upper$ but above the $lower$, the algorithm penalizes\footnote{The idea of penalty comes from our initial exploration, where merging by distance alone could lead to undesirable outcomes.}
elements.sent-2411-167: For example, assume the code "user feedback" is close enough to "soliciting feedback" and "integrating feedback," yet the latter two are far enough apart.
elements.sent-2411-168: If an ACS starts with the latter two codes, they will not be merged.
elements.sent-2411-169: Yet, by adding "user feedback" to the ACS, a straightforward algorithm will merge all three codes into one, losing the nuances between "soliciting" and "integrating." To prevent this, our algorithm penalizes "oversized" merging results.
elements.sent-2411-170: For example, since "user feedback" will likely accumulate too many examples after merging the "soliciting feedback" and "integrating feedback," the distance threshold to merge into it will be stricter (lower).
elements.sent-2411-171: To reduce merging similarly named codes with different intentions, the algorithm also penalizes the differences between codes' examples.

elements.sent-2411-313: Since running 150 evaluation runs for a single comparison study may be cost-ineffective, we evaluated our method's reliability when only one model, and temperature, and 10 evaluation runs are used.
elements.sent-2411-314: Here, we assume the pairwise comparison results of 150 runs as the ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-315: Across all 15 combinations on four metrics and two datasets, we only identified two cases of false positives for a pair with a minuscule mean difference of $density$ (BERTopic vs. Chunk-level Structured, 32.71\% vs. 32.41\%) in Dataset 1.
elements.sent-2411-316: Besides that, we only identified false negatives for comparison pairs with small mean differences.
elements.sent-2411-317: In other words, unless the goal is to compare metrics with small numerical differences, 10 runs would be generally sufficient.
elements.sent-2411-318: While using GAI in our method inevitably introduces stochasticity and potential biases, we established the reliability of our computational measurement with the reliability study (\ref{reliability-study ).}
elements.sent-2411-319: Our method's only source of stochasticity comes from GAI's generation of code definitions and labels, which influences the downstream decision of code merging.
elements.sent-2411-320: Naturally, coding results with fewer codes will be impacted more, and our empirical results support this assumption.
elements.sent-2411-321: In plain words, our method provides more stable outcomes at evaluating “better” coding results but less so for “worse” ones.
elements.sent-2411-322: This is especially true for $novelty$, which measures the number of codes that no one else has identified.
elements.sent-2411-323: Therefore, we only recommend using $novelty$ as a qualitative indicator for potential outliers, while using $divergence$, $coverage$, and $density$ for quantitative comparisons.
elements.sent-2411-324: We confirmed that using a single LLM and temperature does not generally lead to false reports on paired comparisons.
elements.sent-2411-325: In only one case, 10 runs with a single LLM and temperature caused a false positive against the entirety of 150 runs.
elements.sent-2411-326: Moreover, the result shows that our method does not rely on the most powerful models.

elements.sent-2411-374: A central method in this process is inductive coding, where researchers identify and interpret codes directly from the datasets themselves.
elements.sent-2411-375: Yet, this exploratory approach poses challenges for meeting methodological expectations (such as ``depth'' and ``variation''), especially as researchers increasingly adopt Generative AI (GAI) for support.
elements.sent-2411-376: Ground-truth-based metrics are insufficient because they contradict the exploratory nature of inductive coding, while manual evaluation can be labor-intensive.
elements.sent-2411-377: This paper presents a theory-informed computational method for measuring inductive coding results from humans and GAI.
elements.sent-2411-378: Our method first merges individual codebooks using an LLM-enriched algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-379: It measures each coder's contribution against the merged result using four novel metrics: Coverage, Overlap, Novelty, and Divergence.
elements.sent-2411-380: Through two experiments on a human-coded online conversation dataset, we 1) reveal the merging algorithm's impact on metrics; 2) validate the metrics' stability and robustness across multiple runs and different LLMs; and 3) showcase the metrics' ability to diagnose coding issues, such as excessive or irrelevant (hallucinated) codes.
elements.sent-2411-381: Our work provides a reliable pathway for ensuring methodological rigor in human-AI qualitative analysis.
elements.sent-2411-382: Qualitative analysis is widely adopted across many social science disciplines.
elements.sent-2411-383: Most often, qualitative researchers apply descriptive labels (codes) in two ways: deductive coding, where codes are applied according to a preconceived coding scheme, and inductive coding (``open coding''), where codes are concepts derived from the raw data.
elements.sent-2411-384: While methodologies such as Grounded Theory (GT) \cite{corbin_chapter_2008, corbin_grounded_1990 and Thematic Analysis (TA) \cite{braun_using_2006, terry_thematic_2017 rely on the inductive approach to discover emergent patterns from human data, inductive coding is hampered by its subjective and time-consuming nature \cite{attride-stirling_thematic_2001, bowman_using_2023 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-385: Since ``ground truth'' may not exist at this stage, truth-based evaluation methods (e.g., inter-coder reliability) mismatch inductive coding's inherent open-endedness \cite{mcdonald_reliability_2019, corbin_chapter_2008, terry_thematic_2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-386: As many computational linguistics, machine learning, or qualitative research studies (e.g., \citeauthor{xiao_supporting_2023 ) attempt to leverage Generative AI (GAI) for inductive coding tasks, a theory-informed and computationally operational evaluation method is urgently needed.}
elements.sent-2411-387: This paper introduces a theory-informed computational method for systematically measuring open coding results from both human and machine coders.
elements.sent-2411-388: We propose an LLM-enhanced merging algorithm and four team-based evaluation metrics that do not rely on ground-truth assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-389: We conducted two empirical experiments to 1) reveal the merging algorithm’s impact on the metrics; 2) validate the metrics’ stability and robustness across multiple runs and different LLMs; and 3) showcase the metrics’ ability to diagnose coding issues, such as excessive or irrelevant (hallucinated) codes.
elements.sent-2411-390: The results illustrate the metrics' stability, robustness, and use cases, with caution notes regarding their limitations and potential risks.
elements.subsection-2411-008: The Nature and Challenges of Inductive Qualitative Coding
elements.sent-2411-391: During inductive coding, researchers identify concepts and themes directly from raw data \cite{strauss_basics_1998, rahman_advantages_2016 , aiming at discovering novel insights often without an existing theoretical framework \cite{corbin_chapter_2008, corbin_grounded_1990, strauss_basics_1998, terry_thematic_2017 .}}
elements.sent-2411-392: The process involves iterating through a corpus to identify meaningful segments and assign descriptive labels (codes) that emerge directly from the data.
elements.sent-2411-393: Researchers then often group their coded segments into labeled ``categories'' that are used for further analysis.
elements.sent-2411-394: This process resembles iterative clustering in machine learning contexts.
elements.sent-2411-395: Inductive coding is open-ended, subjective, and does not strive for a singular ``correct'' result \cite{terry_thematic_2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-396: Rather, the process should capture as many aspects, patterns, or ``codable moments'' as possible \cite{terry_thematic_2017, corbin_grounded_1990, corbin_chapter_2008-1 .}
elements.sent-2411-397: Yet, inductive coding is inherently subjective, time-consuming, and prone to ambiguities, making methodological rigor difficult to achieve \cite{attride-stirling_thematic_2001, bowman_using_2023, braun_one_2021, bringer_maximizing_2004, tuckett_applying_2005, furniss_confessions_2011, saunders_saturation_2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-398: Since the process aims to widely capture novel insights rather than enforcing consistency, deductive coding metrics (such as inter-rater reliability) become gravely inadequate due to their reliance on ``ground truth'' assumptions \cite{mcdonald_reliability_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-399: To address this mismatch, qualitative researchers are shifting towards team-based approaches, where the team constantly compares and contrasts codes from multiple individuals \cite{cascio_team-based_2019, thomas_general_2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-400: Team-based approaches embrace different perspectives, resulting in more insights and mitigating individual biases \cite{corbin_grounded_1990, thomas_general_2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-401: It makes researchers closer towards the elusive goals of inductive analysis, such as depth, variation, and theoretical saturation \cite{corbin_chapter_2008, adams_qualitative_2008, furniss_confessions_2011, saunders_saturation_2018 .}
elements.subsection-2411-009: Evaluating ML/GAI for Inductive Qualitative Coding
elements.sent-2411-402: ML/GAI approaches offer significant potential to support and enhance qualitative research by assisting in the coding process \cite{xiao_supporting_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-403: Existing computational approaches have primarily framed machine-assisted qualitative coding as either a classification-based task, which mimics human labels, or a generation-based task, which produces codes directly from data \cite{liew_optimizing_2014, gebreegziabher_patat_2023, rietz_cody_2021, xiao_supporting_2023, grootendorst2022bertopic, saravani2023automated, sievert2014ldavis, de_paoli_can_2023, sinha_role_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-404: Effectively leveraging ML/GAI's potential requires robust evaluation methods that account for the open-ended and exploratory characteristics of inductive coding, yet existing ones are largely insufficient:.
elements.sent-2411-405: While it provides quantifiable metrics such as precision and recall, its presupposition of a single correct answer directly contradicts qualitative research theories \cite{corbin_chapter_2008, terry_thematic_2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-406: Essentially, this approach constrains inputs to a predefined scope, thereby limiting the discovery of novel insights \cite{liew_optimizing_2014, xiao_supporting_2023, parfenova2025text .}
elements.sent-2411-407: Topic coherence metrics measure the consistency of topic word representation, evaluating the interpretability and meaningfulness of a topic \cite{rahimi2023contextualized .}
elements.sent-2411-408: It works without a ground truth and instead examines the internal latent properties of topics.
elements.sent-2411-409: However, as inductive coding often aims to identify at a more nuanced and crosscutting level than topics or themes \cite{corbin_chapter_2008 , the method's usefulness is limited.}
elements.sent-2411-410: Human-annotated evaluations ask experts to assess the usefulness, explainability, or relevance of machine-generated codes through instruments such as survey forms \cite{de_paoli_can_2023, de_paoli_performing_2023, zambrano_ncoder_2023, spinoso2023qualitative .}
elements.sent-2411-411: However, such annotations are labor-intensive and may still fail to detect systemic biases, such as the consistent omission of critical codes \cite{parfenova2025text .}
elements.sent-2411-412: Building on the team-based approach adopted by qualitative researchers \cite{thomas_general_2006 , our novel method aggregates multiple coders' coding results (i.e., codebooks) to calculate four computational metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-413: The calculation of these metrics does not rely on ground truth(s) or human inputs.
elements.sent-2411-414: That said, we still recommend using expert coding results as anchor points, especially when measuring coding results from untested models or prompts.
elements.sent-2411-415: A reference implementation of the aggregation method, documentation, and the calculation of our metrics is released to the general public (see \href{https://github.com/CIVITAS-John/LLM-Qualitative-Analysis {the open-sourced software package , licensed under CC BY-NC 4.0).}}
elements.subsection-2411-010: Aggregating Coding Results: Code Spaces (CSP) and Aggregated Code Spaces (ACS)
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[height=120pt]{figures/acs-csp.png} \caption{A: A conceptual illustration of an ACS merged from $csp_1$ and $csp_2$. B: Measuring $csp_1$ using the merged ACS as a reference.} \label{fig:acs-and-csp}
elements.sent-2411-416: The first step towards calculating our proposed metrics is to aggregate the codebooks produced by multiple individual coders into a single conceptual space that serves as an approximation of ``all possible interpretations'' of the data, as prescribed by qualitative analysis methods (Fig. \ref{fig:acs-and-csp ).}
elements.sent-2411-417: To do this, we first consider each coder's results and define their Code Space (CSP), to be the set of all codes they identified or interpreted from the dataset (Fig. \ref{fig:acs-and-csp A).}
elements.sent-2411-418: In turn, we can consider the union of all individual CSPs as the Aggregate Code Space (ACS) that encompasses the codes identified or interpreted from the dataset by all coders.
elements.sent-2411-419: However, simply considering the ACS to be this union does not take into account that real-world coders often use different codes to represent identical or very similar ideas (e.g., the codes ``User Feedback'' vs ``Feedback from User'').
elements.sent-2411-420: To account for this, we propose a four-stage algorithm that utilizes semantic similarity and hierarchical clustering to iteratively consolidate an ACS from a set of individual CSPs.
elements.sent-2411-421: In addition to this, we also expand our definition of an ACS to include the fact that semantically similar codes are connected by links as $Neighbors$ (Fig. \ref{fig:acs-and-csp B).}
elements.sent-2411-422: Noting that each code in an individual CSP has a $label$ (the code itself) and may have $examples$ (pieces of data the code was applied to), and a $definition$ (a description of the concept the code covers), the algorithm is thus defined as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-423: Begin with the ACS simply being the union of all individual CSPs, only considering the $label$ of each code.
elements.sent-2411-424: For each code in the ACS, use hierarchical clustering to merge codes with semantically very similar $labels$.
elements.sent-2411-425: For each merging pair, the shorter label will be adopted.
elements.sent-2411-426: For each code in the ACS, use LLMs to generate a new $definition$ based on its $label$ and $examples$.
elements.sent-2411-427: Then, repeat the merging using both $label$ and $definition$, using LLMs to generate the resulting $label$ and $definition$ of the merged code.
elements.sent-2411-428: Finally, repeat stage 3 but iteratively and using our modified clustering algorithm (see below for more details).
elements.sent-2411-429: In stages 2 and 3, we apply a strict threshold for cosine distances between the text embeddings of each code.
elements.sent-2411-430: However, we found that a single threshold was often insufficient in separating different codes.
elements.sent-2411-431: In stage 4, we therefore adapt the hierarchical clustering algorithm to apply two thresholds ($lower$ and $upper$) on each node of the dendrogram, from which the $penalty$ coefficient is calculated.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: [H] \caption{First Penalty on the Difference Between Examples} \label{alg:distance} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require For each code in $ACS$, a normalized text embedding $C = \{c_1, \dots, c_n\}$. \State $e = \frac{|E_A \cap E_B|}{|E_A \cup E_B|}$ \State $dist_{a, b} = d(c_a, c_b) + penalty * e^2$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: [H] \caption{Second Penalty on Unique Examples of the Potential Merge} \label{alg:merge} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require $dist$ = adjusted distance of the node \Require $count$ unique examples of the node \State $o = max(\frac{count - count_{avg}}{count_{max} - count_{avg}}, 0)$ \If{$dist \le lower$} \State \Return YES \ElsIf{$dist > upper$} \State \Return NO \ElsIf{$dist + penalty * o^2 < upper$} \State \Return YES \Else \State \Return NO \Comment{Considered as ``Neighbors''} \EndIf \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-432: The four metrics that we propose measure each coder's CSP against the ACS in four ways: Coverage, Overlap, Novelty, and Divergence (Fig. \ref{fig:acs-and-csp B).}
elements.sent-2411-433: Coverage: How much conceptual space does a given CSP cover in the ACS? Both TA and GT strive for ``richness'' of codes, capturing depth and variation for further analysis \cite{corbin_chapter_2008, braun2013successful .}
elements.sent-2411-434: Keeping in mind that not all codes are equally interesting, each code is weighted by the number of coders who identified it.
elements.sent-2411-435: To mitigate the impact of a given coder applying an excessive number of codes to the data, each coder is weighted by the number of codes they identified.
elements.algorithm-2411-003: [H] \caption{Weighting Codes and Codebooks} \label{alg:weighting} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \For{$x \in ACS$} \State $size_x = \max\{\#(code\in x), size_{median}\}$ \State $weight_x = \frac{1}{\ln{(size_x)}}$ \For{$code \in ACS$} \If{$c \in x$} \State $obs_{x,c}$ *= 1 \Else \State $obs_{x,c}$ *= $\frac{\ln{(|neighbors \in x| + 1)}}{\ln{(|neighbors| + 1))}}$ \EndIf \State $score_{c}$ += $obs_{x,c} * weight_x$ \EndFor \EndFor \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-004: [H] \caption{Calculating Coverage} \label{alg:coverage} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \For{$x \in ACS$} \State $coverage_x = \frac{\sum_{c\in acs} obs_{x,c} \times score_{c}}{\sum_{c\in acs} score_{c}}$ \EndFor \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-436: Overlap: How much does a given CSP overlap with others? The algorithm is almost the same as the one proposed for coverage, except each coder's impact on $score$ is removed from the ACS (see Divergence).
elements.sent-2411-437: Novelty: How many ``unique'' codes does a given CSP include (i.e., codes that no other coders identified)? In considering novelty, we measure how much novel conceptual space was covered by a given coder.
elements.algorithm-2411-005: [H] \caption{Calculating Novelty} \label{alg:novelty} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \For{$x \in ACS$} \State $novelty_x = \frac{\sum_{c\in csp (novel = 1)} obs_{x, c} * score_c}{\sum_{c\in acs (novel = 1)} score_c}$ \EndFor \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-438: Divergence: How far is a given CSP's code distribution from the ACS? We calculate each CSP's divergence as the separation from its probability distribution from that of other CSPs.
elements.sent-2411-439: We used the Jensen-Shannon Divergence (JSD) to tolerate potential zeros.
elements.algorithm-2411-006: [H] \caption{Calculating Divergence} \label{alg:divergence} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \For{$x \in ACS$} \State $B_c = score_c - obs_{x,c}*weight_x$\Comment Baseline - excluding the coder's contribution \State $divergence_x = \sqrt{JSD(B \parallel obs_{x,c})}$ \EndFor \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-440: To empirically validate our computational metrics and merging algorithm, we designed two experiments to answer three research questions (RQ1-RQ3):.
elements.sent-2411-441: How does each stage of our merging algorithm affect our metrics?.
elements.sent-2411-442: Given the probabilistic nature and different capabilities of LLMs, how robust or stable are our metrics?.
elements.sent-2411-443: Can our metrics identify edge cases such as excessive codes or hallucinations?.
elements.sent-2411-444: We reused the prompt, dataset, and human coding results from \citeauthor{chen2025processes 's study, where researchers conducted manual evaluation.}
elements.sent-2411-445: In each experiment, we applied our computational metrics to inductive qualitative coding results from four human coders (three PhD students, one undergraduate student, all in a U.S.
elements.sent-2411-446: Both experiments work on an online conversation dataset between Physics Lab (an online learning software)'s designers and teacher users.
elements.sent-2411-447: The conversation happened in public messaging groups, and the collection of such data has been approved by a university IRB.
elements.sent-2411-448: The dataset is attached as part of the Software package and has been properly anonymized.
elements.sent-2411-449: Our usage of the dataset is consistent with the original intention and IRB approval.
elements.sent-2411-450: Due to human researchers' limited capacity, we focused on the first 127 messages.
elements.sent-2411-451: The same question was provided to human and machine coders: ``How did Physics Lab's online community emerge?'' Since most qualitative data are from human subjects and are subject to IRB protection, we intentionally chose open-source and locally available models for the experiments.
elements.sent-2411-452: We used Gemma3-27B (with temperature = 0.5) to generate new sets of machine codes.
elements.sent-2411-453: We used mxbai-embed-large to calculate semantic distances \cite{emb2024mxbai, li2023angle .}
elements.sent-2411-454: Experiment 1 addresses RQ1 and RQ2 through an ablation study on the four stages of our merging algorithm .
elements.sent-2411-455: We chose four machine coders from \citeauthor{ 's comparison study: Chunk-Level (i.e., generate per ``chunk'' of messages); Chunk-Level, Structured; Item-Level (i.e., generate per message); Item-Level, Verb Phrases Only.}
elements.sent-2411-456: Condition 1 corresponds to the first ``naive'' stage, where codes are merged solely by their labels.
elements.sent-2411-457: Condition 2 corresponds to the second stage, where codes are merged with a strict threshold (0.32) by their labels.
elements.sent-2411-458: Condition 3 corresponds to the third stage, where an LLM generates definitions based on each code's label and examples.
elements.sent-2411-459: Then, the codes are merged with a strict threshold (0.32) by labels and definitions.
elements.sent-2411-460: Condition 4 corresponds to the fourth stage, where the codes are iteratively merged with an upper threshold (0.55) and a lower threshold (0.32) by labels and definitions, until no more codes can be merged.
elements.sent-2411-461: For each condition and LLM used, we repeated 10 runs, recorded each human and machine coder's number of codes within the merged ACS, and calculated four computational metrics.
elements.sent-2411-462: In addition to individual coders, we also calculated metrics for the combination ``group'' of AI or human coders.
elements.sent-2411-463: Thresholds in each condition are chosen interactively through our example implementation.
elements.sent-2411-464: The strict threshold is chosen by ensuring that 10 code pairs with a semantic distance right below it have the same meanings.
elements.sent-2411-465: The upper threshold is chosen by ensuring that 10 code pairs right below it have at least similar meanings.
elements.sent-2411-466: In Stages 3 and 4, the study doubles as a comparison between different LLMs used in the process: Gemma3 27B (non-reasoning, small, open-source) \cite{gemma_2025 , Qwen QwQ 32B (reasoning, small, open-source) \cite{qwq32b , GPT-4.1 (non-reasoning, large, proprietary), and Gemini-2.5-Pro (reasoning, large, proprietary).}}
elements.sent-2411-468: Starting from the Item-Level coder, which performed the best (together with its Verb Phrases Only Variant) in human evaluation and computational metrics, we created three variants to simulate potential edge cases:.
elements.sent-2411-469: Flooding Coder is explicitly instructed to generate an excessive number of codes per item.
elements.sent-2411-470: Hallucinating Coder has the same prompt but works with an irrelevant, AI-generated conversation.
elements.sent-2411-471: Hallucinate + Flooding Coder combines the two changes together.
elements.sent-2411-472: For each variant, we repeated 10 runs with the same human coders and machine coders, replacing results from the Item-Level coder with its variant.
elements.sent-2411-473: Since our preliminary results find little impact on LLM choice, we only used Gemma3 27B for this experiment.
elements.sent-2411-474: In total, both experiments cost \~8 million LLM tokens (5 read, 3 write), around \$20 for proprietary models.

elements.subsection-2411-015: RQ1: Measuring Each Stage's Impact on Our Merging Algorithm
elements.sent-2411-477: Experiment 1 first examines how each stage of our merging algorithm impacts each coder's number of merged codes and the resulting metrics.
elements.subsubsection-2411-020: Hypothesis 1: Evaluation condition significantly affects the number of merged codes and computed metrics.
elements.sent-2411-478: We used ordinary least squares (OLS) regression to model the effect of algorithmic stages (Conditions 1-4) on the number of consolidated codes and each coder's four metrics.. Result: Mostly Confirmed.
elements.sent-2411-479: Each algorithm stage significantly reduced the total number of merged codes ($p < 0.001$).
elements.sent-2411-480: As shown in Table \ref{tab:h1-reg-coefs , we observed significant shifts in computational metrics in Conditions 3 and 4, but not in 2.}

Conditions 1 and 2 are deterministic. For other values, p < 0.001.} \label{tab:h1-reg-coefs}
elements.subsubsection-2411-021: Hypothesis 2: Evaluation condition has minimal impact on the relative ranking of coder metrics.
elements.sent-2411-481: We conducted a ranking stability analysis across conditions using one-way ANOVA with Tukey HSD post-hoc comparisons.. Result: Partially Confirmed.

elements.subsection-2411-016: RQ2: Evaluating the Robustness and Stability of Our Proposed Metrics
elements.sent-2411-485: Experiment 1 then evaluates whether our computational metrics remain robust across repeated runs and different LLMs in Conditions 3 and 4.
elements.subsubsection-2411-022: Hypothesis 3: LLM used in the merging process significantly influences metrics and code counts.
elements.sent-2411-486: We used OLS regression to model the effect of LLMs on the number of consolidated codes and each coder's four metrics, controlling for fixed effects between Conditions 3 and 4 and between individual coders.. Result: Partially Confirmed.
elements.sent-2411-487: Across Conditions 3 and 4, three models (Gemma3 27B, Qwen QwQ 32B, and GPT 4.1) produce very similar metrics and numbers of merged codes.
elements.sent-2411-488: The only substantial deviation comes from Gemini-2.5-pro, which produces fewer merged codes, higher coverage and overlap (approximately 4\% to 6\% increase), and lower divergence (4\% decrease) (Fig.~\ref{fig:merging-llm-effects ).}

Each bar represents the average coefficient difference from the grand mean across LLMs, along with 95\% confidence intervals. } \label{fig:merging-llm-effects}
elements.subsubsection-2411-023: Hypothesis 4: Metric outcomes are well-explained by condition, model, and coder identity.
elements.sent-2411-489: From the same OLS model used by Hypothesis 3, we calculated adjusted~$R^2$ values to determine the extent to which the combination of condition, merging LLM, and coder explains variation in metric values.. Result: Confirmed.
elements.subsubsection-2411-024: Hypothesis 5: Repeated measurements under the same condition/model yield low coefficients of variation (CoV).
elements.sent-2411-491: We calculated the coefficient of variation for each metric over 10 evaluation runs per Condition per merging LLM.. Result: Confirmed.

elements.subsection-2411-017: RQ3: Testing Our Proposed Metrics' Diagnostic Utility for Edge Cases
elements.sent-2411-498: Experiment 2 tests whether our computational metrics can detect abnormal inductive qualitative codes from machine coder variants designed to simulate edge cases: Flooding, Hallucinating, and Combined (Fig. \ref{fig:edge-cases ).}
elements.figure-2411-014: [H] \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/edgecases-metrics.png} \caption{Mean coder metrics across Baseline, Flooding, Hallucinating, and Combined variants with 95\% confidence intervals.} \label{fig:edge-cases}
elements.subsubsection-2411-026: Hypothesis 7: Excessive coding increases coverage, overlap, and novelty with diminishing returns, while divergence remains stable.
elements.sent-2411-499: The mean metrics from the flooding coder have higher coverage (78.7\%), overlap (57.9\%), and novelty (68.1\%) than the Baseline coder.
elements.sent-2411-502: A similar effect is observed between the Hallucinating and Combined coders.
elements.subsubsection-2411-027: Hypothesis 8: Coding on irrelevant data (i.e., hallucination) reduces coverage and overlap, while increasing divergence.
elements.sent-2411-503: The mean metrics from the Hallucinating coder have reduced coverage (35.6\%) and overlap (15.6\%), while divergence increases sharply to 75.7\%.
elements.sent-2411-504: A stronger effect is observed between the Hallucinating and Combined coders.
elements.sent-2411-505: In particular, while the Combined coder produced 245\% more codes than the baseline (1,775 vs. 514), it has lower overlap (39.6\% vs. 25.2\%) and higher divergence (76.3\% vs. 64.9\%).
elements.sent-2411-506: This paper introduces a theory-informed computational method for systematically evaluating the outputs of inductive coding.
elements.sent-2411-507: At the heart of our contribution are four metrics designed to capture the multifaceted nature of inductive coding:.
elements.sent-2411-508: Coverage measures the depth and variation of a coder's contribution against the Aggregated Code Space (ACS), reflecting the qualitative goal of achieving breadth and depth in analysis \cite{corbin_chapter_2008 .}
elements.sent-2411-509: Overlap quantifies a coder's alignment with the conceptual consensus of the group, indicating how much their interpretations resonate with others.
elements.sent-2411-510: Novelty identifies the unique concepts a coder introduces, highlighting their potential value in bringing new perspectives to the analytical process.
elements.sent-2411-511: Divergence measures how much a coder's conceptual focus differs from others in the group, offering insight into their unique analytical lens.
elements.sent-2411-512: Taken together, these metrics provide a holistic and nuanced assessment of a coder's performance that does not rely on ``ground truths'' that may not exist even with teams of human experts.
elements.sent-2411-513: Instead of pursuing a single, simplistic measure of agreement, our method embraces the subjectivity and exploratory spirit of inductive qualitative analysis \cite{corbin_grounded_1990, braun_thematic_2012 .}
elements.sent-2411-514: It enables researchers to appreciate not only consensus but also the valuable variation that different coders bring to the table, providing a more complete picture of human-AI or human-human collaboration processes.
elements.subsection-2411-018: The Necessity of the Iterative, LLM-Enriched Merging Algorithm
elements.sent-2411-515: RQ1 explored the impact of each stage of our merging algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-516: The findings from our ablation study (see \ref{rq1 ) show that while each stage progressively reduces the number of codes in the Aggregated Code Space (ACS), the most significant shift in our metrics occurs between Stage 2 and Stage 3.}
elements.sent-2411-517: This transition, which introduces LLM-generated definitions to the merging process, is far more impactful than simply relaxing semantic distance thresholds, as seen in the transitions between other stages.
elements.sent-2411-518: This result confirms a central premise of our work: capturing true conceptual similarity in inductive coding requires moving beyond superficial linguistic parallels.
elements.sent-2411-519: The ``naive'' merging stages (Conditions 1 and 2), which rely solely on code labels, are insufficient.
elements.sent-2411-520: Therefore, the introduction of LLM-generated definitions centrally contributes to our proposed metrics.
elements.sent-2411-521: This stage infuses the semantic representation of each code with meaning derived from its underlying data points (``examples''), without forcing a direct comparison between those data points.
elements.sent-2411-522: Such insulation is vital for the inductive workflow, where coders may legitimately identify the same concept in different segments of the data \cite{corbin_grounded_1990 ; comparing data points directly would be prone to error.}
elements.sent-2411-523: Hypotheses 1 and 2 also clarify the distinct roles of Stage 4 (iteratively merging with more relaxed thresholds), which produces a highly consolidated ACS but does not significantly impact the measuring outcome.
elements.sent-2411-524: Since we regenerate a label and definition for each merging pair, Stage 4 is computationally intensive.
elements.sent-2411-525: Therefore, its utility depends on the research goal.
elements.sent-2411-526: To produce a clean conceptual map for further interpretation, comparison, or synthesis (e.g., when identifying a coder's potential bias), the computational cost of Stage 4 is well justified.
elements.sent-2411-527: Conversely, when numerical metrics are the primary output (such as in large-scale model comparisons), the less computationally demanding Stage 3 may provide sufficiently stable and reliable outcomes for screening purposes.
elements.subsection-2411-019: The Impact of LLM on Outcomes
elements.sent-2411-528: While the involvement of LLMs in the merging processes introduces intrinsic randomness, the findings from RQ2 confirm our method's reliability and robustness across multiple LLMs and repeated runs.
elements.sent-2411-529: As shown in Hypothesis 4, with very high adjusted $R^2$ values, our proposed metrics are overwhelmingly explained by coders' intrinsic merits.
elements.sent-2411-530: Repeated measurements under the same conditions yield low coefficients of variation (Hypothesis 5), showing stability across multiple LLM samplings.
elements.sent-2411-531: Crucially, our metrics' stability extends to the choice of LLM.
elements.sent-2411-532: Tested on a diverse set of models, the choice of model has little effect on the relative ranking of coders (Hypothesis 6), where the top and bottom-ranked performers remained consistent across all LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-533: This implication is significant for the practical adoption by qualitative researchers, as they can confidently use smaller, open-source models (e.g., Gemma3 27B) to measure outcomes from human or machine coders.
elements.sent-2411-534: Such models can be easily deployed locally, providing better privacy protections and regulation compliance for handling often sensitive human subject data.
elements.subsection-2411-020: Performance of Metrics in Edge Cases
elements.sent-2411-535: RQ3 evaluated the robustness and diagnostic utility of our metrics in simulated edge cases.
elements.sent-2411-536: The ``Flooding'' coder variant, explicitly prompted to produce an excessive number of codes, was expected to create significant redundancy.
elements.sent-2411-537: The metrics correctly capture that: both ``Flooding'' variants registered higher Coverage and Novelty, but with diminishing returns that mark the results' redundancy.
elements.sent-2411-538: Similarly, the ``Hallucinating'' coder variant, which worked from irrelevant data with the correct prompt, was expected to produce thematically similar codes but fail to capture critical details from the true data.
elements.sent-2411-539: The metrics capture the issue as well: both ``Hallucinating'' variants produced a predictable and dramatic drop in Coverage and Overlap, coupled with a sharp increase in Divergence.
elements.sent-2411-540: In all three cases, our metrics behave predictably in response to those coding issues, suggesting that abnormal comparative values from our metrics can serve as a ``red flag'' for further evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-541: This diagnostic capability offers a layer of quality control that is essential for ensuring rigor in human-AI collaborative workflows.
elements.sent-2411-542: This paper presents a reliable and robust computational method for the theory-informed, systematic evaluation of inductive coding.
elements.sent-2411-543: By moving beyond a reliance on a single ``ground truth,'' our approach provides a practical pathway for qualitative researchers to leverage AI in inductive analytical processes responsibly.
elements.sent-2411-544: By measuring and quantifying conceptual ideas like coverage, overlap, novelty, and divergence, we shift the evaluation focus of inductive coding from enforcing agreement to a more nuanced appreciation of the diverse contributions that each coder brings to the collaborative, exploratory process.
elements.sent-2411-545: As more and more qualitative researchers explore LLMs for inductive coding, we offer a timely contribution to ensure methodological rigor and facilitate more effective and transparent human-AI collaboration.

elements.sent-2411-552: While this study presents a robust computational method for evaluating inductive coding, it is important to acknowledge its limitations.
elements.sent-2411-553: From there, future research can help establish best practices and clear guidelines for qualitative researchers to adopt our metrics.
elements.subsection-2411-021: Limitation on the Dataset and Domain
elements.sent-2411-554: The validation of our metrics was conducted on a single dataset consisting of online conversations from a specific online community.
elements.sent-2411-555: While effective in this context, the method's performance and the metrics' utility need to be tested on more diverse forms of qualitative data, such as semi-structured interview transcripts or open-ended survey responses.
elements.sent-2411-556: The nuances of these different data types may present unique challenges not encountered in this study.
elements.subsection-2411-022: Limitation on the Simulated Edge Cases
elements.sent-2411-557: The ``Flooding'' and ``Hallucinating'' coders were created through explicit instructions to simulate poor coding practices.
elements.sent-2411-558: Although our metrics successfully identified these simulated cases, further research is needed to validate their diagnostic capabilities in real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-559: This includes testing the method on codes generated by novice human researchers, non-expert coders, or subtly flawed AI prompts, which may produce less extreme and harder-to-detect issues than our simulated variants.
elements.subsection-2411-023: Limited Interpretations on Deviant LLM Behaviors
elements.sent-2411-560: Despite 3 out of 4 models behaving similarly in our experiment (Hypothesis 3), our findings observed deviated merging behaviors and behaviors with Gemini-2.5-Pro, which showed a tendency to merge codes more.
elements.sent-2411-561: Even when the ranking order is relatively stable, this model choice leads to significant changes in raw metric numbers.
elements.sent-2411-562: Further research should investigate whether and/or why the stronger reasoning model merges differently, and whether this deep merging may prove preferable or not to human researchers.
elements.sent-2411-563: While our computational method is designed to mitigate risks in human-AI qualitative analysis, its improper application or the misinterpretation of its metrics can introduce other potential risks.
elements.sent-2411-564: Especially when coming from a non-qualitative research background, users may treat our computational metrics as objective, definitive measures of quality, contrary to the exploratory and subjective nature of inductive coding.
elements.sent-2411-565: For example, when substituting a baseline machine coder with its deviant variants, we interpreted the higher Divergence metrics as an indicator of potential hallucination.
elements.sent-2411-566: However, deviating from the consensus can also be valuable in other scenarios, such as a coder from a different intellectual tradition or lived background.
elements.sent-2411-567: Over-reliance on metric values can prevent junior researchers from developing the critical, interpretive skills that come from manual comparison, reflection, and building consensus with peers.
elements.sent-2411-568: Hence, our metrics should be used as diagnostic tools to prompt deeper qualitative inquiry, not as a substitute for it.
elements.sent-2411-569: The Aggregated Code Space (ACS) is fundamentally a synthesis of the input codebook group.
elements.sent-2411-570: The quality and comprehensiveness of the evaluation are therefore dependent on the diversity and rigor of the coding team.
elements.sent-2411-571: If the entire human-AI team shares a particular bias or overlooks a critical theme, the ACS will reflect this omission.
elements.sent-2411-572: Consequently, researchers who rely solely on metric values risk automation bias, which diminishes critical engagement with the raw data and the coding process.
elements.sent-2411-573: We encourage researchers to continue recruiting a diverse human team and employing multiple LLMs for inductive coding.
elements.sent-2411-574: Moreover, they should also explore the interactive interface generated by our software package, which provides a network-based visualization of codes and metrics.
elements.sent-2411-575: We intend to further study the interface in an upcoming research project.
elements.sent-2411-576: Our method involves processing data through Large Language Models and text embedding models.
elements.sent-2411-577: While our study prioritized the use of local, open-source models to protect sensitive data, researchers applying this method with proprietary, the usage of cloud-based APIs risk exposing confidential or personally identifiable information from their datasets.
elements.sent-2411-578: It is hence critical for researchers to maintain adherence to IRB protocols and relevant data privacy regulations.
elements.sent-2411-579: This appendix provides complete regression tables supporting Hypothesis 1 (Section~\ref{h1 ), which tests whether the algorithmic condition significantly affects the number of consolidated codes and the quality of generated open codes.}
elements.sent-2411-580: We report outputs from five ordinary least squares (OLS) models.
elements.sent-2411-581: Regression 1 models the number of consolidated codes per transcript as a function of condition.
elements.sent-2411-582: Regression 2 consists of four separate models, each predicting one code-level metric: coverage, overlap, novelty, or divergence.
elements.sent-2411-583: Conditions are dummy-coded, and coder identity is sum-coded.
elements.sent-2411-584: All regressions use the design matrix described in Section~\ref{h1 , with coder identity sum-coded, condition dummy-coded (Condition~1 as the baseline), and heteroscedasticity-robust (HC3) standard errors.}

elements.sent-2411-591: This appendix providesOLS regression results supporting Hypotheses 3 and 4 (Section~\ref{h3 and~\ref{h4 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-592: Hypothesis~3 tests whether the LLM used in the merging process significantly influences each code-level metric and the number of consolidated codes.
elements.sent-2411-593: Hypothesis~4 evaluates how well condition, merging model, and coder identity together explain variation in outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-594: All results are based on the model: \begin{equation}Y \sim C(condition) + C(model, Sum) + C(coder, Sum)\end{equation} where $Y$ represents each outcome of interest.
elements.sent-2411-595: We use dummy-coding for the condition (Condition 3 as the baseline), sum-coding for the model and coder identity (Average as the baseline), and HC3 heteroscedasticity-robust standard errors.
elements.sent-2411-596: Coefficients are interpreted as deviations from the baseline.
